3	require "active_support/core_ext/module/delegation"
4	
5	module ActiveRecord
6	  module Delegation # :nodoc:
7	    class << self
8	      def delegated_classes
9	        [
10	          ActiveRecord::Relation,
11	          ActiveRecord::Associations::CollectionProxy,
12	          ActiveRecord::AssociationRelation,
13	          ActiveRecord::DisableJoinsAssociationRelation,
14	        ]
15	      end
16	
17	      def uncacheable_methods
18	        @uncacheable_methods ||= (
19	          delegated_classes.flat_map(&:public_instance_methods) - ActiveRecord::Relation.public_instance_methods
20	        ).to_set.freeze
21	      end
22	    end
23	
24	    module DelegateCache # :nodoc:
25	      def relation_delegate_class(klass)
26	        @relation_delegate_cache[klass]
27	      end
28	
29	      def initialize_relation_delegate_cache
30	        @relation_delegate_cache = cache = {}
31	        Delegation.delegated_classes.each do |klass|
32	          delegate = Class.new(klass) {
33	            include ClassSpecificRelation
34	          }
35	          include_relation_methods(delegate)
36	          mangled_name = klass.name.gsub("::", "_")
37	          const_set mangled_name, delegate
38	          private_constant mangled_name
39	
40	          cache[klass] = delegate
41	        end
42	      end
43	
44	      def inherited(child_class)
45	        child_class.initialize_relation_delegate_cache
46	        super
47	      end
48	
49	      def generate_relation_method(method)
50	        generated_relation_methods.generate_method(method)
51	      end
52	
53	      protected
54	        def include_relation_methods(delegate)
55	          superclass.include_relation_methods(delegate) unless base_class?
56	          delegate.include generated_relation_methods
57	        end
58	
59	      private
60	        def generated_relation_methods
61	          @generated_relation_methods ||= GeneratedRelationMethods.new.tap do |mod|
62	            const_set(:GeneratedRelationMethods, mod)
63	            private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods
64	          end
65	        end
66	    end
67	
68	    class GeneratedRelationMethods < Module # :nodoc:
69	      MUTEX = Mutex.new
70	
71	      def generate_method(method)
72	        MUTEX.synchronize do
73	          return if method_defined?(method)
74	
75	          if /\A[a-zA-Z_]\w*[!?]?\z/.match?(method) && !::ActiveSupport::Delegation::RESERVED_METHOD_NAMES.include?(method.to_s)
76	            module_eval <<-RUBY, __FILE__, __LINE__ + 1
77	              def #{method}(...)
78	                scoping { klass.#{method}(...) }
79	              end
80	            RUBY
81	          else
82	            define_method(method) do |*args, **kwargs, &block|
83	              scoping { klass.public_send(method, *args, **kwargs, &block) }
84	            end
85	          end
86	        end
87	      end
88	    end
89	    private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods
90	
91	    extend ActiveSupport::Concern
92	
93	    # This module creates compiled delegation methods dynamically at runtime, which makes
94	    # subsequent calls to that method faster by avoiding method_missing. The delegations
95	    # may vary depending on the klass of a relation, so we create a subclass of Relation
96	    # for each different klass, and the delegations are compiled into that subclass only.
97	
98	    delegate :to_xml, :encode_with, :length, :each, :join, :intersect?,
99	             :[], :&, :|, :+, :-, :sample, :reverse, :rotate, :compact, :in_groups, :in_groups_of,
100	             :to_sentence, :to_fs, :to_formatted_s, :as_json,
101	             :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
102	
103	    delegate :primary_key, :lease_connection, :connection, :with_connection, :transaction, to: :klass
104	
105	    module ClassSpecificRelation # :nodoc:
106	      extend ActiveSupport::Concern
107	
108	      module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
109	        def name
110	          superclass.name
111	        end
112	      end
113	
114	      private
115	        def method_missing(method, ...)
116	          if @klass.respond_to?(method)
117	            unless Delegation.uncacheable_methods.include?(method)
118	              @klass.generate_relation_method(method)
119	            end
120	            scoping { @klass.public_send(method, ...) }
121	          else
122	            super
123	          end
124	        end
125	    end
126	
127	    module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
128	      def create(klass, *args, **kwargs)
129	        relation_class_for(klass).new(klass, *args, **kwargs)
130	      end
131	
132	      private
133	        def relation_class_for(klass)
134	          klass.relation_delegate_class(self)
135	        end
136	    end
137	
138	    private
139	      def respond_to_missing?(method, _)
140	        super || @klass.respond_to?(method)
141	      end
142	  end
143	end

3	module ActiveRecord
4	  module Associations
5	    # = Active Record Collection Proxy
6	    #
7	    # Collection proxies in Active Record are middlemen between an
8	    # <tt>association</tt>, and its <tt>target</tt> result set.
9	    #
10	    # For example, given
11	    #
12	    #   class Blog < ActiveRecord::Base
13	    #     has_many :posts
14	    #   end
15	    #
16	    #   blog = Blog.first
17	    #
18	    # The collection proxy returned by <tt>blog.posts</tt> is built from a
19	    # <tt>:has_many</tt> <tt>association</tt>, and delegates to a collection
20	    # of posts as the <tt>target</tt>.
21	    #
22	    # This class delegates unknown methods to the <tt>association</tt>'s
23	    # relation class via a delegate cache.
24	    #
25	    # The <tt>target</tt> result set is not loaded until needed. For example,
26	    #
27	    #   blog.posts.count
28	    #
29	    # is computed directly through SQL and does not trigger by itself the
30	    # instantiation of the actual post records.
31	    class CollectionProxy < Relation
32	      def initialize(klass, association, **) # :nodoc:
33	        @association = association
34	        super klass
35	
36	        extensions = association.extensions
37	        extend(*extensions) if extensions.any?
38	      end
39	
40	      def target
41	        @association.target
42	      end
43	
44	      def load_target
45	        @association.load_target
46	      end
47	
48	      # Returns +true+ if the association has been loaded, otherwise +false+.
49	      #
50	      #   person.pets.loaded? # => false
51	      #   person.pets.records
52	      #   person.pets.loaded? # => true
53	      def loaded?
54	        @association.loaded?
55	      end
56	      alias :loaded :loaded?
57	
58	      ##
59	      # :method: select
60	      #
61	      # :call-seq:
62	      #   select(*fields, &block)
63	      #
64	      # Works in two ways.
65	      #
66	      # *First:* Specify a subset of fields to be selected from the result set.
67	      #
68	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
69	      #     has_many :pets
70	      #   end
71	      #
72	      #   person.pets
73	      #   # => [
74	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
75	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
76	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
77	      #   #    ]
78	      #
79	      #   person.pets.select(:name)
80	      #   # => [
81	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Fancy-Fancy">,
82	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Spook">,
83	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Choo-Choo">
84	      #   #    ]
85	      #
86	      #   person.pets.select(:id, :name)
87	      #   # => [
88	      #   #      #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy">,
89	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook">,
90	      #   #      #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo">
91	      #   #    ]
92	      #
93	      # Be careful because this also means you're initializing a model
94	      # object with only the fields that you've selected. If you attempt
95	      # to access a field except +id+ that is not in the initialized record you'll
96	      # receive:
97	      #
98	      #   person.pets.select(:name).first.person_id
99	      #   # => ActiveModel::MissingAttributeError: missing attribute 'person_id' for Pet
100	      #
101	      # *Second:* You can pass a block so it can be used just like <tt>Array#select</tt>.
102	      # This builds an array of objects from the database for the scope,
103	      # converting them into an array and iterating through them using
104	      # <tt>Array#select</tt>.
105	      #
106	      #   person.pets.select { |pet| /oo/.match?(pet.name) }
107	      #   # => [
108	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
109	      #   #      #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
110	      #   #    ]
111	
112	      # Finds an object in the collection responding to the +id+. Uses the same
113	      # rules as ActiveRecord::FinderMethods.find. Returns ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound
114	      # error if the object cannot be found.
115	      #
116	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
117	      #     has_many :pets
118	      #   end
119	      #
120	      #   person.pets
121	      #   # => [
122	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
123	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
124	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
125	      #   #    ]
126	      #
127	      #   person.pets.find(1) # => #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>
128	      #   person.pets.find(4) # => ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound: Couldn't find Pet with 'id'=4
129	      #
130	      #   person.pets.find(2) { |pet| pet.name.downcase! }
131	      #   # => #<Pet id: 2, name: "fancy-fancy", person_id: 1>
132	      #
133	      #   person.pets.find(2, 3)
134	      #   # => [
135	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
136	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
137	      #   #    ]
138	      def find(*args)
139	        return super if block_given?
140	        @association.find(*args)
141	      end
142	
143	      ##
144	      # :method: first
145	      #
146	      # :call-seq:
147	      #   first(limit = nil)
148	      #
149	      # Returns the first record, or the first +n+ records, from the collection.
150	      # If the collection is empty, the first form returns +nil+, and the second
151	      # form returns an empty array.
152	      #
153	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
154	      #     has_many :pets
155	      #   end
156	      #
157	      #   person.pets
158	      #   # => [
159	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
160	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
161	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
162	      #   #    ]
163	      #
164	      #   person.pets.first # => #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>
165	      #
166	      #   person.pets.first(2)
167	      #   # => [
168	      #   #      #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
169	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>
170	      #   #    ]
171	      #
172	      #   another_person_without.pets          # => []
173	      #   another_person_without.pets.first    # => nil
174	      #   another_person_without.pets.first(3) # => []
175	
176	      ##
177	      # :method: second
178	      #
179	      # :call-seq:
180	      #   second()
181	      #
182	      # Same as #first except returns only the second record.
183	
184	      ##
185	      # :method: third
186	      #
187	      # :call-seq:
188	      #   third()
189	      #
190	      # Same as #first except returns only the third record.
191	
192	      ##
193	      # :method: fourth
194	      #
195	      # :call-seq:
196	      #   fourth()
197	      #
198	      # Same as #first except returns only the fourth record.
199	
200	      ##
201	      # :method: fifth
202	      #
203	      # :call-seq:
204	      #   fifth()
205	      #
206	      # Same as #first except returns only the fifth record.
207	
208	      ##
209	      # :method: forty_two
210	      #
211	      # :call-seq:
212	      #   forty_two()
213	      #
214	      # Same as #first except returns only the forty second record.
215	      # Also known as accessing "the reddit".
216	
217	      ##
218	      # :method: third_to_last
219	      #
220	      # :call-seq:
221	      #   third_to_last()
222	      #
223	      # Same as #last except returns only the third-to-last record.
224	
225	      ##
226	      # :method: second_to_last
227	      #
228	      # :call-seq:
229	      #   second_to_last()
230	      #
231	      # Same as #last except returns only the second-to-last record.
232	
233	      # Returns the last record, or the last +n+ records, from the collection.
234	      # If the collection is empty, the first form returns +nil+, and the second
235	      # form returns an empty array.
236	      #
237	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
238	      #     has_many :pets
239	      #   end
240	      #
241	      #   person.pets
242	      #   # => [
243	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
244	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
245	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
246	      #   #    ]
247	      #
248	      #   person.pets.last # => #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
249	      #
250	      #   person.pets.last(2)
251	      #   # => [
252	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
253	      #   #      #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
254	      #   #    ]
255	      #
256	      #   another_person_without.pets         # => []
257	      #   another_person_without.pets.last    # => nil
258	      #   another_person_without.pets.last(3) # => []
259	      def last(limit = nil)
260	        load_target if find_from_target?
261	        super
262	      end
263	
264	      # Gives a record (or N records if a parameter is supplied) from the collection
265	      # using the same rules as ActiveRecord::FinderMethods.take.
266	      #
267	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
268	      #     has_many :pets
269	      #   end
270	      #
271	      #   person.pets
272	      #   # => [
273	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
274	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
275	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
276	      #   #    ]
277	      #
278	      #   person.pets.take # => #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>
279	      #
280	      #   person.pets.take(2)
281	      #   # => [
282	      #   #      #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
283	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>
284	      #   #    ]
285	      #
286	      #   another_person_without.pets         # => []
287	      #   another_person_without.pets.take    # => nil
288	      #   another_person_without.pets.take(2) # => []
289	      def take(limit = nil)
290	        load_target if find_from_target?
291	        super
292	      end
293	
294	      # Returns a new object of the collection type that has been instantiated
295	      # with +attributes+ and linked to this object, but have not yet been saved.
296	      # You can pass an array of attributes hashes, this will return an array
297	      # with the new objects.
298	      #
299	      #   class Person
300	      #     has_many :pets

300	      #     has_many :pets
301	      #   end
302	      #
303	      #   person.pets.build
304	      #   # => #<Pet id: nil, name: nil, person_id: 1>
305	      #
306	      #   person.pets.build(name: 'Fancy-Fancy')
307	      #   # => #<Pet id: nil, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>
308	      #
309	      #   person.pets.build([{name: 'Spook'}, {name: 'Choo-Choo'}, {name: 'Brain'}])
310	      #   # => [
311	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
312	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>,
313	      #   #      #<Pet id: nil, name: "Brain", person_id: 1>
314	      #   #    ]
315	      #
316	      #   person.pets.size  # => 5 # size of the collection
317	      #   person.pets.count # => 0 # count from database
318	      def build(attributes = {}, &block)
319	        @association.build(attributes, &block)
320	      end
321	      alias_method :new, :build
322	
323	      # Returns a new object of the collection type that has been instantiated with
324	      # attributes, linked to this object and that has already been saved (if it
325	      # passes the validations).
326	      #
327	      #   class Person
328	      #     has_many :pets
329	      #   end
330	      #
331	      #   person.pets.create(name: 'Fancy-Fancy')
332	      #   # => #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>
333	      #
334	      #   person.pets.create([{name: 'Spook'}, {name: 'Choo-Choo'}])
335	      #   # => [
336	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
337	      #   #      #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
338	      #   #    ]
339	      #
340	      #   person.pets.size  # => 3
341	      #   person.pets.count # => 3
342	      #
343	      #   person.pets.find(1, 2, 3)
344	      #   # => [
345	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
346	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
347	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
348	      #   #    ]
349	      def create(attributes = {}, &block)
350	        @association.create(attributes, &block)
351	      end
352	
353	      # Like #create, except that if the record is invalid, raises an exception.
354	      #
355	      #   class Person
356	      #     has_many :pets
357	      #   end
358	      #
359	      #   class Pet
360	      #     validates :name, presence: true
361	      #   end
362	      #
363	      #   person.pets.create!(name: nil)
364	      #   # => ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid: Validation failed: Name can't be blank
365	      def create!(attributes = {}, &block)
366	        @association.create!(attributes, &block)
367	      end
368	
369	      # Replaces this collection with +other_array+. This will perform a diff
370	      # and delete/add only records that have changed.
371	      #
372	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
373	      #     has_many :pets
374	      #   end
375	      #
376	      #   person.pets
377	      #   # => [#<Pet id: 1, name: "Gorby", group: "cats", person_id: 1>]
378	      #
379	      #   other_pets = [Pet.new(name: 'Puff', group: 'celebrities')]
380	      #
381	      #   person.pets.replace(other_pets)
382	      #
383	      #   person.pets
384	      #   # => [#<Pet id: 2, name: "Puff", group: "celebrities", person_id: 1>]
385	      #
386	      # If the supplied array has an incorrect association type, it raises
387	      # an ActiveRecord::AssociationTypeMismatch error:
388	      #
389	      #   person.pets.replace(["doo", "ggie", "gaga"])
390	      #   # => ActiveRecord::AssociationTypeMismatch: Pet expected, got String
391	      def replace(other_array)
392	        @association.replace(other_array)
393	      end
394	
395	      # Deletes all the records from the collection according to the strategy
396	      # specified by the +:dependent+ option. If no +:dependent+ option is given,
397	      # then it will follow the default strategy.
398	      #
399	      # For <tt>has_many :through</tt> associations, the default deletion strategy is
400	      # +:delete_all+.
401	      #
402	      # For +has_many+ associations, the default deletion strategy is +:nullify+.
403	      # This sets the foreign keys to +NULL+.
404	      #
405	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
406	      #     has_many :pets # dependent: :nullify option by default
407	      #   end
408	      #
409	      #   person.pets.size # => 3
410	      #   person.pets
411	      #   # => [
412	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
413	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
414	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
415	      #   #    ]

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my final report.

## Thorough Understanding of ActiveRecord::Relation Lazy Loading

Based on my examination of the Rails ActiveRecord codebase, here's a comprehensive analysis of how lazy loading works:

### Core Loading Mechanism

**File: `/home/bench/repos/rails/activerecord/lib/active_record/relation.rb`**

**1. Instance Variables (lines 77-88)**
- `@loaded` (line 81): Boolean flag tracking if records have been loaded
- `@records` (line 85): Cached array of instantiated model instances
- `@arel` (reset at line 1184): Cached AST representation of the query (NOT cached as instance var, but reset)
- `@future_result` (line 84): For async query results

**2. The `loaded?` Method (line 74)**
- Simply an alias: `alias :loaded? :loaded`
- Returns the `@loaded` flag directly

**3. The `records` Method (lines 335-338)**
```ruby
def records # :nodoc:
  load
  @records
end
```
This is the **primary trigger for lazy loading**. Every access to `records` calls `load` first, which will execute queries if not already loaded.

**4. The `load` Method (lines 1165-1172)** - EXPLICIT QUERY TRIGGER
```ruby
def load(&block)
  if !loaded? || scheduled?
    @records = exec_queries(&block)
    @loaded = true
  end
  self
end
```
- Checks if `!loaded?` or if a scheduled async query exists (`scheduled?`)
- Calls `exec_queries` which performs the actual database query
- Sets `@loaded = true` after records are retrieved
- **Important**: Can receive a block that's passed to `exec_queries`

**5. The `exec_queries` Method (lines 1385-1403)** - ACTUAL QUERY EXECUTION
```ruby
def exec_queries(&block)
  skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
    rows = if scheduled?
      future = @future_result
      @future_result = nil
      future.result
    else
      exec_main_query
    end
    records = instantiate_records(rows, &block)
    preload_associations(records) unless skip_preloading_value
    records.each(&:readonly!) if readonly_value
    records.each { |record| record.strict_loading!(strict_loading_value) } unless strict_loading_value.nil?
    records
  end
end
```
Steps:
1. Gets raw database rows via `exec_main_query` (or awaits async result)
2. Instantiates model objects via `instantiate_records`
3. Preloads associations (unless skipped)
4. Sets readonly/strict_loading flags
5. Returns frozen array of records

**6. The `exec_main_query` Method (lines 1405-1435)** - DATABASE INTERFACE
- Returns raw result rows from database
- Handles `@none` flag (returns empty array if relation is negated)
- Checks for contradictions in WHERE clause
- For eager loading: applies join dependency
- Otherwise: executes the relation's AREL directly
- Can accept `async: true` parameter to return FutureResult instead

**7. The `reset` Method (lines 1180-1190)** - CLEARS CACHE
- Sets `@loaded = nil` (marks unloaded)
- Clears `@records = nil`
- Clears `@arel = nil` (the cached query AST)
- Clears `@future_result` (cancels async query if pending)
- Used by `reload` to force re-fetching

---

### Short-Circuit Loading (Methods That Check `loaded?` First)

**File: `/home/bench/repos/rails/activerecord/relation.rb`**

**1. `size` Method (lines 346-352)** - AVOIDS LOADING FOR COUNT
```ruby
def size
  if loaded?
    records.length
  else
    count(:all)  # Runs COUNT(*) query instead
  end
end
```
- If already loaded: returns in-memory array length
- If not loaded: runs a COUNT SQL query (much faster than loading all records)

**2. `empty?` Method (lines 355-363)** - AVOIDS LOADING FOR EXISTENCE
```ruby
def empty?
  return true if @none
  if loaded?
    records.empty?
  else
    !exists?  # Runs EXISTS check query
  end
end
```

**3. `one?` Method (lines 397-403)** - AVOIDS LOADING FOR SINGLE RECORD CHECK
```ruby
def one?(*args)
  return false if @none
  return super if args.present? || block_given?
  return records.one? if loaded?
  limited_count == 1  # Runs LIMIT 2 COUNT query
end
```

**4. `many?` Method (lines 406-412)** - AVOIDS LOADING FOR MULTIPLE RECORD CHECK
```ruby
def many?
  return false if @none
  return super if block_given?
  return records.many? if loaded?
  limited_count > 1  # Runs LIMIT 2 COUNT query
end
```

**5. `compute_cache_version` Method (lines 465-509)** - AVOIDS LOADING FOR CACHE VERSION
```ruby
if loaded?
  size = records.size
  if size > 0
    timestamp = records.map { |record| record.read_attribute(timestamp_column) }.max
  end
else
  # Runs COUNT(*) and MAX(timestamp) SQL query instead
end
```

---

### Short-Circuit Loading in FinderMethods

**File: `/home/bench/repos/rails/activerecord/relation/finder_methods.rb`**

**1. `find_take` Method (lines 583-588)** - TAKES FIRST RECORD
```ruby
def find_take
  if loaded?
    records.first
  else
    @take ||= limit(1).records.first
  end
end
```

**2. `find_take_with_limit` Method (lines 591-596)** - TAKES N RECORDS
```ruby
def find_take_with_limit(limit)
  if loaded?
    records.take(limit)
  else
    limit(limit).to_a
  end
end
```

**3. `find_nth` Method (lines 599-601)** - GETS NTH RECORD
```ruby
def find_nth(index)
  @offsets ||= {}
  @offsets[index] ||= find_nth_with_limit(index, 1).first
end
```

**4. `find_nth_with_limit` Method (lines 604-620)** - GETS N RECORDS STARTING AT OFFSET
```ruby
def find_nth_with_limit(index, limit)
  if loaded?
    records[index, limit] || []
  else
    # Uses OFFSET/LIMIT to fetch just needed records from DB
  end
end
```

**5. `find_nth_from_last` Method (lines 623-634)** - GETS NTH FROM END
```ruby
def find_nth_from_last(index)
  if loaded?
    records[-index]
  else
    # Uses reverse ordering or SQL instead
  end
end
```

**6. `find_last` Method (lines 637-638)** - GETS LAST RECORD(S)
```ruby
def find_last(limit)
  limit ? records.last(limit) : records.last
end
```

**7. `last` Method (lines 203-209)** - GETS LAST WITH SMART LOADING
```ruby
def last(limit = nil)
  return find_last(limit) if loaded? || has_limit_or_offset?
  # Otherwise builds optimized reverse query
end
```

**8. `include?` Method (lines 394-405)** - CHECKS IF RECORD IN COLLECTION
```ruby
if loaded? || offset_value || limit_value || having_clause.any?
  records.include?(record)  # Check in memory
else
  exists?(id)  # Run SQL EXISTS query
end
```

---

### Short-Circuit Loading in Calculations

**File: `/home/bench/repos/rails/activerecord/relation/calculations.rb`**

**1. `pluck` Method (lines 296-303)** - EXTRACTS COLUMN VALUES
```ruby
if loaded? && all_attributes?(column_names)
  result = records.pluck(*column_names)
  # Return cached result
end
# Otherwise run SELECT query for just those columns
```

**2. `pick` Method (lines 370)** - GETS SINGLE VALUE
```ruby
if loaded?
  return pick_from_records(*column_names)
else
  # Runs optimized query
end
```

---

### Delegation to Array Methods

**File: `/home/bench/repos/rails/activerecord/relation/delegation.rb` (lines 98-101)**

These methods are delegated directly to `records`, which **ALWAYS** triggers loading:
```ruby
delegate :to_xml, :encode_with, :length, :each, :join, :intersect?,
         :[], :&, :|, :+, :-, :sample, :reverse, :rotate, :compact, :in_groups, :in_groups_of,
         :to_sentence, :to_fs, :to_formatted_s, :as_json,
         :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
```

**Enumerable Methods Trigger Loading:**
- `each` - iterates all records (via `records` delegation)
- `map { |r| ... }` - calls `each` internally
- `select { |r| ... }` - calls `each` internally
- `length` - calls `records.length` (loads all)

**Alternative:** To avoid loading, use `count` instead of `length`.

---

### Methods That BYPASS Loading Entirely

**1. `to_sql` Method (lines 1196-1207)** - GETS SQL STRING WITHOUT EXECUTING

```ruby
    @loaded = true
  end
  self
end
```

- Calls `exec_queries` which performs the actual database query
- Sets `@loaded = true` after records are retrieved

**5. The `exec_queries` Method (lines 1385-1403)** - ACTUAL QUERY EXECUTION

```ruby
def exec_queries(&block)
  skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
    rows = if scheduled?
      future = @future_result
      @future_result = nil
      future.result
    else
      exec_main_query
    end
    records = instantiate_records(rows, &block)
    preload_associations(records) unless skip_preloading_value
    records.each(&:readonly!) if readonly_value
    records.each { |record| record.strict_loading!(strict_loading_value) } unless strict_loading_value.nil?
    records
  end
end
```

1. Gets raw database rows via `exec_main_query` (or awaits async result)
2. Instantiates model objects via `instantiate_records`
3. Preloads associations (unless skipped)
4. Sets readonly/strict_loading flags
5. Returns frozen array of records

- Handles `@none` flag (returns empty array if relation is negated)

- Otherwise: executes the relation's AREL directly
- Can accept `async: true` parameter to return FutureResult instead

```ruby
      if loaded?

      end
```

    # the Enumerable match the pattern via the case-equality operator (<tt>===</tt>).

    def none?(*args)
      return true if @none

      return super if args.present? || block_given?
      empty?

# If the query was actually executed in the background, the Active Record logs will show
    # it by prefixing the log line with <tt>ASYNC</tt>:
    #
    #   ASYNC Post Load (0.0ms) (db time 2ms)  SELECT "posts".* FROM "posts" LIMIT 100
    def load_async
      with_connection do |c|
        return load if !c.async_enabled?

        unless loaded?
          result = exec_main_query(async: c.current_transaction.closed?)

          if result.is_a?(Array)
            @records = result
          else
            @future_result = result
          end
          @loaded = true
        end
      end

      self
    end

    # Returns <tt>true</tt> if the relation was scheduled on the background
    # thread pool.
    def scheduled?
      !!@future_result
    end

    # Causes the records to be loaded from the database if they have not
    # been loaded already. You can use this if for some reason you need
    # to explicitly load some records before actually using them. The
    # return value is the relation itself, not the records.
    #
    #   Post.where(published: true).load # => #<ActiveRecord::Relation>
    def load(&block)
      if !loaded? || scheduled?
        @records = exec_queries(&block)
        @loaded = true
      end

      self
    end

    # Forces reloading of relation.
    def reload
      reset
      load
    end

    def reset
      @future_result&.cancel
      @future_result = nil
      @delegate_to_klass = false
      @to_sql = @arel = @loaded = @should_eager_load = nil
      @offsets = @take = nil
      @cache_keys = nil
      @cache_versions = nil
      @records = nil
      self
    end

    # Returns sql statement for the relation.
    #
    #   User.where(name: 'Oscar').to_sql
    #   # SELECT "users".* FROM "users"  WHERE "users"."name" = 'Oscar'

      @to_sql ||= if eager_loading?
        apply_join_dependency do |relation, join_dependency|
          relation = join_dependency.apply_column_aliases(relation)
          relation.to_sql
        end
      else
        klass.with_connection do |conn|
          conn.unprepared_statement { conn.to_sql(arel) }
        end
      end
    end

      def exec_queries(&block)
        skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
          rows = if scheduled?
            future = @future_result
            @future_result = nil
            future.result
          else
            exec_main_query
          end

          records = instantiate_records(rows, &block)
          preload_associations(records) unless skip_preloading_value

          records.each(&:readonly!) if readonly_value
          records.each { |record| record.strict_loading!(strict_loading_value) } unless strict_loading_value.nil?

      def exec_main_query(async: false)
        if @none
          if async
            return FutureResult.wrap([])
          else
            return []
          end
        end

        skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
          if where_clause.contradiction?
            [].freeze

            klass.with_connection do |c|

def last(limit = nil)
      return find_last(limit) if loaded? || has_limit_or_offset?

      result = ordered_relation.limit(limit)
      result = result.reverse_order!

      limit ? result.reverse : result.first
    end

    # Same as #last but raises ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound if no record
    # is found. Note that #last! accepts no arguments.
    def last!
      last || raise_record_not_found_exception!
    end

    # Find the second record.
    # If no order is defined it will order by primary key.
    #
    #   Person.second # returns the second object fetched by SELECT * FROM people
    #   Person.offset(3).second # returns the second object from OFFSET 3 (which is OFFSET 4)
    #   Person.where(["user_name = :u", { u: user_name }]).second
    def second
      find_nth 1
    end

    # Same as #second but raises ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound if no record
    # is found.
    def second!
      second || raise_record_not_found_exception!
    end

def include?(record)
      # The existing implementation relies on receiving an Active Record instance as the input parameter named record.
      # Any non-Active Record object passed to this implementation is guaranteed to return `false`.
      return false unless record.is_a?(klass)

      if loaded? || offset_value || limit_value || having_clause.any?
        records.include?(record)
      else
        id = if record.class.composite_primary_key?
          record.class.primary_key.zip(record.id).to_h
        else
          record.id
        end

        exists?(id)
      end
    end

    alias :member? :include?

The key points:
1. The materialization routine: `load` (relation.rb:1165) → `exec_queries` (relation.rb:1385) → `exec_main_query` (relation.rb:1405)
2. Access paths that force materialization: `records` (the central one, line 335), and all the delegation methods in delegation.rb:98-101
3. Access paths that deliberately avoid loading: `size`, `empty?`, `one?`, `many?`, `include?`, `last`, `pluck`, `pick`

content.text:
Here is the complete picture, verified against the source.

---

## The materialization routine

**`load` — `relation.rb:1165`** is the gateway:

```ruby
def load(&block)
  if !loaded? || scheduled?
    @records = exec_queries(&block)
    @loaded = true
  end
  self
end
```

It checks `@loaded` (exposed via `loaded?`). If false — or if an async result is pending — it calls `exec_queries`, stores the result in `@records`, and sets `@loaded = true`. It returns `self`, not the records.

**`exec_queries` — `relation.rb:1385`** does the real work:
1. If `scheduled?` is true (an async background query was started with `load_async`), it awaits `@future_result.result`.
2. Otherwise it calls `exec_main_query`, which runs the SQL via `klass._query_by_sql` (or the join-dependency path for eager loads).
3. Then: instantiate model objects → preload associations → stamp `readonly!` / `strict_loading!`.

**`exec_main_query` — `relation.rb:1405`** short-circuits before touching the database if `@none` is set or `where_clause.contradiction?` is true — both return `[]` immediately.

---

## The one-stop access point

**`records` — `relation.rb:335`**:

```ruby
def records
  load
  @records
end
```

Every path that needs the in-memory array goes through `records`. It always calls `load` first, so `load`'s `!loaded?` guard is the single place that decides whether a SQL round-trip happens.

**`to_ary` / `to_a` — `relation.rb:330`**: both delegate to `records.dup`, so they trigger the same path.

---

## Delegation — the largest set of triggers

`delegation.rb:98–101` wires a fixed list of array methods directly to `records`:

```ruby
delegate :to_xml, :encode_with, :length, :each, :join, :intersect?,
         :[], :&, :|, :+, :-, :sample, :reverse, :rotate, :compact, :in_groups, :in_groups_of,
         :to_sentence, :to_fs, :to_formatted_s, :as_json,
         :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
```

Any call to one of these — including the ubiquitous `each`, `[]`, `length` — unconditionally calls `records`, which calls `load`. There is no `loaded?` check before the delegation; the guard lives inside `load` itself.

---

## Methods that deliberately avoid loading

These all branch on `loaded?` at their own level and, when the relation is cold, fire a narrower SQL query instead of loading every record:

| Method | File | What it does when NOT loaded | What it does when loaded |
|---|---|---|---|
| `size` | `relation.rb:346` | `count(:all)` — runs `COUNT(*)` | `records.length` |
| `empty?` | `relation.rb:355` | `!exists?` — runs `EXISTS` or `COUNT` | `records.empty?` |
| `none?` | `relation.rb:371` | delegates to `empty?` | same |
| `any?` | `relation.rb:384` | delegates to `empty?` | same |
| `one?` | `relation.rb:397` | `limited_count == 1` — `LIMIT 2` count | `records.one?` |
| `many?` | `relation.rb:406` | `limited_count > 1` — `LIMIT 2` count | `records.many?` |
| `include?` | `finder_methods.rb:389` | `exists?(id)` — SQL EXISTS (unless offset/limit/having are set, in which case it forces load) | `records.include?(record)` |
| `last` | `finder_methods.rb:202` | builds a reversed `ORDER BY … LIMIT n` relation and calls `first` on it — only materializes that one row | `find_last` → `records.last` |
| `pluck` | `calculations.rb:296` | runs `SELECT col FROM …` — only fetches named columns | `records.pluck(…)` (in-memory) |
| `pick` | `calculations.rb:349` | `pluck(*cols).first` — single-row narrow select | `records.first.slice(…)` |
| `count` / `sum` / `min` / `max` / `average` | `calculations.rb` | aggregate SQL query; never touches `@records` | aggregate SQL query (ignores cache) |
| `exists?` | `finder_methods.rb` | runs `SELECT 1 FROM … LIMIT 1` | runs the query anyway — does not check `loaded?` |

Note the asymmetry on `exists?`: it never consults `@records` even if the data is already in memory — it always goes to the database.

---

## Finder methods: conditional load

These check `loaded?` and, when cold, issue a targeted query rather than loading everything:

- **`find_nth_with_limit` (`finder_methods.rb:603`)** — if loaded, slices `@records[index, limit]`; if cold, runs `OFFSET index LIMIT limit`.
- **`find_nth_from_last` (`finder_methods.rb:622`)** — if loaded, uses `records[-index]`; if cold, reverses ordering and queries.
- **`find_take` / `find_take_with_limit` (`finder_methods.rb:582/590`)** — if loaded, reads from `@records`; if cold, adds `LIMIT n` and calls `records` (materializing only that slice).

---

## `load_async` — the async fork

`load_async` (`relation.rb:1134`) takes a different path:

```ruby
unless loaded?
  result = exec_main_query(async: c.current_transaction.closed?)
  if result.is_a?(Array)   # async unavailable, got rows directly
    @records = result
  else
    @future_result = result  # FutureResult scheduled in thread pool
  end
  @loaded = true
end
```

It sets `@loaded = true` immediately, before the query finishes. `scheduled?` (`relation.rb:1155`) returns `!!@future_result`. When `load` is later called for real access, the `!loaded? || scheduled?` condition re-enters `exec_queries`, which then waits for `@future_result.result` and promotes it into `@records`.

---

## What never loads

- **`to_sql` (`relation.rb:1196`)** — builds the SQL string from the Arel AST without executing it. Cached in `@to_sql`.
- **`arel`** — builds the Arel AST; no execution.
- All scope-building methods (`where`, `order`, `limit`, `joins`, …) — they return a new Relation object with modified bindings; nothing is executed.

---

## The reset path

`reset` (`relation.rb:1180`) clears everything: cancels `@future_result`, nils out `@loaded`, `@records`, `@arel`, `@to_sql`. `reload` calls `reset` then immediately calls `load`, forcing a fresh query even if the relation was already materialized.

---

**Summary of the decision tree for any method call on a Relation:**

1. Is this a scope-builder (`where`/`order`/…)? → returns a new cold Relation, no query.
2. Is this a delegated Array method (`each`, `[]`, `length`, …)? → calls `records` → calls `load` → queries if `!loaded? || scheduled?`.
3. Is this a smart aggregate (`size`, `empty?`, `count`, `pluck`, …)? → checks `loaded?` itself; uses a narrow SQL query when cold, in-memory operation when warm.
4. Is this `to_sql` or `arel`? → never queries, returns query description only.

```ruby
         :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
```

Any call to one of these — including the ubiquitous `each`, `[]`, `length` — unconditionally calls `records`, which calls `load`. There is no `loaded?` check before the delegation; the guard lives inside `load` itself.

---

## Methods that deliberately avoid loading

These all branch on `loaded?` at their own level and, when the relation is cold, fire a narrower SQL query instead of loading every record:

| Method | File | What it does when NOT loaded | What it does when loaded |
|---|---|---|---|
| `size` | `relation.rb:346` | `count(:all)` — runs `COUNT(*)` | `records.length` |
| `empty?` | `relation.rb:355` | `!exists?` — runs `EXISTS` or `COUNT` | `records.empty?` |
| `none?` | `relation.rb:371` | delegates to `empty?` | same |
| `any?` | `relation.rb:384` | delegates to `empty?` | same |
| `one?` | `relation.rb:397` | `limited_count == 1` — `LIMIT 2` count | `records.one?` |
| `many?` | `relation.rb:406` | `limited_count > 1` — `LIMIT 2` count | `records.many?` |
| `include?` | `finder_methods.rb:389` | `exists?(id)` — SQL EXISTS (unless offset/limit/having are set, in which case it forces load) | `records.include?(record)` |
| `last` | `finder_methods.rb:202` | builds a reversed `ORDER BY … LIMIT n` relation and calls `first` on it — only materializes that one row | `find_last` → `records.last` |
| `pluck` | `calculations.rb:296` | runs `SELECT col FROM …` — only fetches named columns | `records.pluck(…)` (in-memory) |
| `pick` | `calculations.rb:349` | `pluck(*cols).first` — single-row narrow select | `records.first.slice(…)` |
| `count` / `sum` / `min` / `max` / `average` | `calculations.rb` | aggregate SQL query; never touches `@records` | aggregate SQL query (ignores cache) |
| `exists?` | `finder_methods.rb` | runs `SELECT 1 FROM … LIMIT 1` | runs the query anyway — does not check `loaded?` |

Note the asymmetry on `exists?`: it never consults `@records` even if the data is already in memory — it always goes to the database.

---

## Finder methods: conditional load

These check `loaded?` and, when cold, issue a targeted query rather than loading everything:

- **`find_nth_with_limit` (`finder_methods.rb:603`)** — if loaded, slices `@records[index, limit]`; if cold, runs `OFFSET index LIMIT limit`.
- **`find_nth_from_last` (`finder_methods.rb:622`)** — if loaded, uses `records[-index]`; if cold, reverses ordering and queries.
- **`find_take` / `find_take_with_limit` (`finder_methods.rb:582/590`)** — if loaded, reads from `@records`; if cold, adds `LIMIT n` and calls `records` (materializing only that slice).

---

## `load_async` — the async fork

`load_async` (`relation.rb:1134`) takes a different path:

```ruby
unless loaded?
  result = exec_main_query(async: c.current_transaction.closed?)
  if result.is_a?(Array)   # async unavailable, got rows directly
    @records = result
  else
    @future_result = result  # FutureResult scheduled in thread pool
  end
  @loaded = true
end
```

It sets `@loaded = true` immediately, before the query finishes. `scheduled?` (`relation.rb:1155`) returns `!!@future_result`. When `load` is later called for real access, the `!loaded? || scheduled?` condition re-enters `exec_queries`, which then waits for `@future_result.result` and promotes it into `@records`.

---

## What never loads

- **`to_sql` (`relation.rb:1196`)** — builds the SQL string from the Arel AST without executing it. Cached in `@to_sql`.
- **`arel`** — builds the Arel AST; no execution.
- All scope-building methods (`where`, `order`, `limit`, `joins`, …) — they return a new Relation object with modified bindings; nothing is executed.

---

## The reset path

`reset` (`relation.rb:1180`) clears everything: cancels `@future_result`, nils out `@loaded`, `@records`, `@arel`, `@to_sql`. `reload` calls `reset` then immediately calls `load`, forcing a fresh query even if the relation was already materialized.

---

**Summary of the decision tree for any method call on a Relation:**

1. Is this a scope-builder (`where`/`order`/…)? → returns a new cold Relation, no query.
2. Is this a delegated Array method (`each`, `[]`, `length`, …)? → calls `records` → calls `load` → queries if `!loaded? || scheduled?`.
3. Is this a smart aggregate (`size`, `empty?`, `count`, `pluck`, …)? → checks `loaded?` itself; uses a narrow SQL query when cold, in-memory operation when warm.
4. Is this `to_sql` or `arel`? → never queries, returns query description only.
